key_topics: Course administration and module structure (12-unit and 6-unit modules; 36-lecture requirement)

Quantum mechanics anecdote: getting an A by learning only the high points
Guest segment: Simone on NSF SBIR, customer discovery/validation, pipeline testing startup
Guest segment: Steve Lyons on intellectual property as gap in MIT curriculum
Guest segment: Brian Holman on da Vinci as war engineer (1450s–1500s)
Etymology of 'engineer' (French; maker of war machines); first US engineering schools (West Point 1797, Norwich 1821, RPI 1823)
BMW i3 graphite-fiber body; cost structure of automobiles (health insurance #1, frame #2, seats #3)
TQM as 'BS or real' — IBM University Challenge anecdote
Math textbooks 'quantized' in two-page lumps — SAT-prep critique

Pole vault poles (sheet composite, stiffness gradient — past student presentation example)
Oil & gas transmission pipeline NDT (Simone's company)

editorial_flags: p4: 'Bell Mars' — proper noun captioner garble; tentative correction to 'Belmares' bracketed. Editor to confirm guest lecturer's name.

p12: Tom claims Vera Kistiakowsky's father won the Nobel Prize in chemistry at Harvard. George Kistiakowsky (her father, Harvard chem dept chair, Manhattan Project) was not a Nobel laureate. Tom's factual slip preserved per Convention §16; flag for layer 3 footnote.

p19–p23: Simone's segment has multiple captioner-non-native-speaker interactions producing garbled text (e.g., 'hope in the houses' likely 'open houses'). Preserved verbatim in layer 2; layer 3 will require editorial smoothing or excerpt-only treatment.
p33: Tom says 'Rensselaer Polytechnic University' — correct name is 'Institute' (RPI). Slip preserved.
p35, p43: Tom genuinely loses his train of thought and reads garbled slide text aloud. Preserved as in-the-moment teaching business per Convention §2 spirit.
notes: This is an unusual recitation: course-introduction day with three guest lecturers (Simone, Steve Lyons, Brian Holman) framing their own forthcoming modules. The technical content is light; the lecture's value is in (a) Eagar's pedagogy statements (no quizzes, takeaway summaries, scientist-vs-engineer quotes), (b) the math-textbook-quantized and quantum-mechanics anecdotes, (c) the brief case mentions (BMW i3, pole vault poles, TQM/IBM). Layer 3 sectioning should respect speaker boundaries: Tom's opening administrative remarks, Tom on pedagogy and scientist/engineer, Simone segment, Tom on guest speakers and BMW i3, Steve Lyons segment, Tom + Brian Holman segment, Tom on engineering history and Harvard, Tom on grading and the quantum mechanics anecdote, Tom on TQM, closing.
